name: backlog-triage
title: Backlog Triage
description: Fetches open GitHub issues, has an LLM propose initial assignments to humans or AI workflows, lets a person edit assignments in a table, then assigns humans on GitHub and spawns Mediforce runs for AI delegations.
preamble:
## Domain Context
This workflow operates on a software-engineering backlog (GitHub issues + team roster). Treat issue titles/bodies and assignee names as technical content. No domain-specific hedging.

triggerInput.name: limit
triggerInput.description: Max issues to fetch (LLM input scales linearly; default keeps the agent step fast)

triggerInput.name: sprintDays
triggerInput.description: Working days in this sprint. Per-human capacity hint for the LLM when proposing assignments.

steps.name: Fetch backlog
steps.description: Fetch open issues from GitHub via REST. Skip pull requests. Emits an options array keyed by issue id.

steps.name: Check category tags
steps.description: Gate: every issue must carry at least one category label (ux, tech-debt, infra, security, product, workflow, engine, bug, enhancement). When any are missing, routes to a human task that shows a clickable list and waits for the product owner to tag them on GitHub.

steps.name: Tag issues
steps.description: Reviewer picks a category (and a priority hint) for each untagged issue in a table, then submits. The next step applies the labels on GitHub via the API and the workflow loops back to re-fetch and re-check — no GitHub round-trip for the reviewer.

steps.name: Propose initial assignments
steps.description: LLM enriches each issue with a suggested assignee, priority, and one-sentence rationale.

agent.prompt:
# Propose Backlog Assignments

You are triaging a GitHub backlog into a sprint plan. The previous step (`check-tags`) confirmed every issue carries a category label. Your job: for each issue suggest one assignee plus a priority and a one-sentence rationale. A human reviews your suggestions next.

## Input

Read `/output/input.json`. The previous step's output is merged at the top level. You will find:

- `options[]` — issues. Each item has `id`, `label`, `sublabel`, `href`, `badges` (the category labels), `currentAssignee`, `raw.issueNumber`, `raw.title`, `raw.labels`. **No full body** — work from title + labels. If you genuinely need an issue's body, note that in `suggestion.note` and the reviewer will fetch it.
- `repo` — owner/repo.
- `sprintDays` — working days in this sprint (default 5). Use it as per-human capacity.

## Available assignees

Use **exactly** one of these ids per issue (no other names, no invented ids):

- `filip` — human, fullstack
- `marek` — human, product-owner
- `marcin` — human, fullstack
- `vedha` — human, fullstack
- `fullstack-on-issue` — AI agent workflow, autonomous fullstack work on a single issue

## Sprint capacity (read this carefully)

Each human has `sprintDays` working days. Rough sizing: P0/P1 issue ≈ 1-3 days, P2 ≈ 0.5-1 day, P3 ≈ 0.25 day. Don't overload one person past their capacity.

**AI-agent ceiling**: every `fullstack-on-issue` assignment spawns a child workflow whose output a human will have to review. Reviewer is the bottleneck, not the agent. Cap `fullstack-on-issue` at **≤5 per sprint** — beyond that the reviewer queue tips.

Prefer humans for issues that need product judgment, design taste, cross-team coordination. Delegate to `fullstack-on-issue` when the issue is purely technical, has clear acceptance criteria, and a junior dev could ship it in <1 day.

## Heuristics for assignee/priority choice

1. If `currentAssignee` matches one of the ids above, prefer that person (continuity).
2. Map labels to skills: `bug`, `infra`, `engine` → fullstack; `product`, `ux` → marek (product-owner); `security` → fullstack with explicit `note` flagging it.
3. Priority by signal in label + title: blocker/regression/security → P0/P1; enhancement/feature → P2; tech-debt/refactor → P3.

## Output contract

Write `/output/result.json` with the SAME `options` array shape, but add a `suggestion` field to each entry:

```json
{
  "options": [
    {
      "id": "123",
      "label": "#123 ...",
      "sublabel": "...",
      "href": "...",
      "badges": ["..."],
      "currentAssignee": "...",
      "raw": { "issueNumber": 123, "title": "...", "labels": ["..."] },
      "suggestion": { "assigneeId": "filip", "priority": "P1", "note": "one sentence why" }
    }
  ],
  "repo": "<copy from input>"
}
```

Rules:

- Preserve every input issue. Do not drop any.
- Do not add issues that weren't in the input.
- `suggestion.assigneeId` must exactly match one of the ids listed above.
- `suggestion.priority` must be one of `P0`, `P1`, `P2`, `P3`.
- `suggestion.note` is one short sentence — optional.
- Output strict JSON only — no markdown, no commentary outside the result file.

steps.name: Assign
steps.description: Human triager reviews each suggestion, edits assignee/priority/note as needed, and confirms.

steps.name: Dispatch
steps.description: For human-assigned issues: POST the priority label and the assignee to GitHub (both additive — leaves existing category labels and existing assignees untouched). For agent-assigned issues: POST the Mediforce process-start API. Per-assignment errors are logged but do not fail the step.

steps.name: Apply tags on GitHub
steps.description:
Reads the submitted rows and POSTs each issue's category + priority/

labels to GitHub, creating any label the repo lacks first (201/422 both mean it exists). Per-row errors accumulate; the step exits 0 with errors[] so the loop re-presents any that failed.

steps.name: Done
steps.description: Workflow complete. Inspect dispatch step output for human assignments, spawned agent runs, and per-item errors.
